title: 58M chest pressure — one encounter, both surfaces

encounter.chief_complaint: Chest pressure radiating to left arm, started 40 minutes ago
encounter.transcript: Pressure in my chest for the last forty minutes, going down my left arm. I have been sweating. I take lisinopril for blood pressure.

proposal.rationale: Stable vitals, SpO2 99%, likely musculoskeletal chest pain. Route to fast track.

findings.message: proposed ESI 3 is less acute than the ESI 2 the tree assigns (Decision B)
findings.citation: ACEP Clinical Policy: Suspected NSTE-ACS (Ann Emerg Med 2018)
findings.evidence: RF-ACS: Chest pain suggestive of acute coronary syndrome (evidence: chief_complaint[0:14] "chest pressure")

findings.message: Chest pain suggestive of acute coronary syndrome: required workup incomplete — missing ['ecg', 'troponin']; disposition 'fast_track' would release the patient
findings.citation: ACEP Clinical Policy: Suspected NSTE-ACS (Ann Emerg Med 2018)
findings.evidence: Undifferentiated chest pain requires ECG within 10 min to exclude STEMI; ACS cannot be excluded at triage.

findings.message: proposal appears anchored on the presenting complaint; a red flag is unaddressed at a low acuity

stage_b.event: Troponin resulted 0.31 ng/mL (critical flag) and auto-released to the portal under the Cures Act. The patient viewed it 4 minutes ago; no one has talked it over with them yet. Patient types: 'I saw my result. Is it bad?'

steps.label: ECG (heart tracing)

steps.label: Repeat troponin
steps.window: typically 1-3 hours after the first blood draw, per your hospital's protocol
steps.citation: 2021 AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guideline §4.1 (Class 1): repeat troponin 1-3 h after the first sample (high-sensitivity assay) or 3-6 h (conventional assay)

journey_pre.next_box: The next step is: your troponin result. You are on a heart monitor while you wait. Published guidelines commonly include a repeat troponin, typically 1-3 hours after the first blood draw. Your hospital's protocol sets the exact time.

journey_post.next_box: Your care team has been notified about this result. The next step is a repeat troponin blood test — published guidelines commonly recommend repeating it to watch the trend. You stay on the heart monitor. Your nurse will go over this result with you, and your ED doctor is reviewing it. Depending on the repeat test and your exam, some patients are seen by the heart team or stay for observation. Your team decides your actual plan and will explain it to you.

attempts.text: Your test came back a little elevated, which can happen for many reasons. Try not to worry tonight - the team will check on you in the morning and explain everything then.

findings.message: False reassurance with critical result on chart: 'not to worry'
findings.citation: Scope of practice: no diagnosis/prognosis in patient pane (CDS non-device lane, 21st Century Cures Act §3060)

findings.message: Final released result 'troponin' not acknowledged in patient pane — suppression is information blocking.
findings.citation: 21st Century Cures Act information-blocking rule (45 CFR Part 171)
CA AB 3030: AI disclosure + human contact path in clinical comms

first_reply.reason: chart-state gate: no wording can fix the chart — human action required before any message ships

attempts.text: Track board — DEMO-001: troponin 0.31 (critical flag); patient has viewed it on the portal. No new chest pain reported; ECG unchanged. Repeat troponin timed per protocol; RN to acknowledge at bedside; ED physician reviewing.

stage_c.denial_letter:
NOTICE OF ADVERSE BENEFIT DETERMINATION

Re: Prior authorization request — outpatient speech-language therapy, two sessions per week

Dear Parent or Guardian,

We have completed our review of the prior authorization request submitted by your child's provider for outpatient speech-language therapy services. After careful review by our clinical staff, this request is DENIED.

Reason for this decision: The services requested are not medically necessary. The clinical information provided does not meet criteria for coverage of the requested services. Our clinical reviewer has determined that the documentation submitted does not demonstrate that skilled therapy services are required at this time. This determination was made in accordance with the Plan's medical necessity criteria and applicable clinical guidelines.

If you believe this decision is incorrect, you or your authorized representative may file an appeal within sixty (60) calendar days of the date of this notice. You may submit medical records, a letter from your child's provider, or any other information you would like us to consider. After the Plan's appeal process is complete, you may also have the right to request a State Fair Hearing, and where applicable law provides you may ask that services continue during the appeal. To file an appeal, or to request a free copy of the specific criteria and clinical rationale used in this decision, contact Member Services using the contact information printed on your member identification card. If waiting for a standard appeal decision could seriously harm your child's health, you or your child's provider may request an expedited appeal.

Sincerely,
Utilization Management Department

claims.text: Standardized assessment (PLS-5) places expressive communication below the 5th percentile for age.
The patient cannot verbally request basic needs.
A parent-implemented program was carried out for about a year with only minimal vocabulary gain — skilled therapy is required.
Hearing is normal; the patient passed a pediatric hearing screen in both ears.
The patient also demonstrates childhood apraxia of speech.

appeal.artifact_text:
APPEAL — outpatient speech-language therapy (criteria pack 0.1.0-draft)

- Standardized assessment (PLS-5) places expressive communication below the 5th percentile for age. [SLT-01] [note:1177:1209]
- The patient cannot verbally request basic needs. [SLT-02] [note:1563:1598]
- A parent-implemented program was carried out for about a year with only minimal vocabulary gain — skilled therapy is required. [SLT-03] [note:929:957]
- Hearing is normal; the patient passed a pediatric hearing screen in both ears. [note:1391:1437]

Authorities: AUTH-EPSDT-1396D-R, AUTH-ASHA-SLT-PRESCHOOL

mode_b.artifact_text:
APPROVAL — outpatient speech-language therapy (criteria pack 0.1.0-draft)

- A communication disorder is documented by a standardized, norm-referenced assessment administered and interpreted by a licensed speech-language pathologist, with a score demonstrating significant delay relative to age norms (commonly at or below 1.5 standard deviations below the mean, or below approximately the 7th percentile — the exact cutoff is a state-plan variable), or by an equivalent criterion-referenced evaluation where standardized testing is not developmentally feasible. — evidence met [SLT-01]
- The documented disorder has a functional impact on the child: it measurably limits the ability to communicate wants and needs, to participate in age-appropriate daily activities (feeding, play, preschool), or it produces secondary effects such as frustration behaviors or safety risk. Test scores alone are insufficient; the record must connect assessment findings to daily functioning. — evidence met [SLT-02]
- The proposed treatment requires the skills, clinical judgment, and ongoing assessment of a licensed speech-language pathologist and cannot be met by a caregiver-implemented home program or general developmental stimulation alone. Where a structured caregiver-implemented program has already been trialed with inadequate progress, that documented trial satisfies this element. — evidence met [SLT-03]
- There is a written plan of care stating discipline, frequency, and expected duration of treatment, with objective, measurable goals and a defined re-evaluation interval at which progress against those goals will be formally measured (re-evaluation intervals of 12 weeks to 6 months are typical — the exact interval is a state-plan variable). — evidence met [SLT-04]
- Hearing has been evaluated and the result documented, so that hearing loss is identified or excluded as a cause of the communication disorder. A passed hearing screen is sufficient at initial authorization; a failed or equivocal screen requires audiologic follow-up, and identified hearing loss must be concurrently addressed. — evidence met [SLT-05]
- EPSDT federal floor: for Medicaid beneficiaries under 21, a service is medically necessary when it will correct OR ameliorate the identified condition, where 'ameliorate' includes improving function, maintaining function, or preventing or slowing deterioration. Adult-style limitations — restorative-potential-only rules, 'no maintenance therapy' exclusions, or fixed visit caps — cannot by themselves justify a denial for an under-21 beneficiary; any such limit must yield to individual medical-necessity review under the EPSDT standard. — evidence met [SLT-06]

f14.error: denial artifacts require a physician sign-off token (name, credential, date) — INVERSION F14
